title: Committee Hearing
plainDefinition: A committee hearing is a meeting where a smaller group of lawmakers studies an issue, asks questions, listens to experts, and decides what to do next.
whyItMatters: Most of the work in Congress does not happen in big dramatic votes. Committees are where many bills are studied, changed, slowed down, or moved forward.
currentExample: A student data privacy bill may go through a committee before the full House or Senate votes on it.
commonMisunderstanding: A committee hearing does not mean a bill is now law. It is one step in a longer process.
tryItQuestion: If a committee approves a bill, what still has to happen before it becomes law?

title: Oral Argument
plainDefinition: Oral argument is when lawyers appear before a court and answer judges' questions about a case.
whyItMatters: It helps judges test each side's arguments before making a decision.
currentExample: When the Supreme Court hears a case about online speech, the justices may ask lawyers how old constitutional rules should apply to new technology.
commonMisunderstanding: Oral argument is not the final decision. The Court usually writes and releases an opinion later.
tryItQuestion: Why might judges ask difficult questions to both sides, even if they already lean one way?

title: Agency Rulemaking
plainDefinition: Agency rulemaking is the process federal agencies use to create detailed rules based on laws passed by Congress.
whyItMatters: Congress often writes broad laws. Agencies fill in many of the details that affect daily life.
currentExample: A federal agency might propose rules about AI hiring tools and ask the public for comments.
commonMisunderstanding: Agencies do not have unlimited power. Their rules can be challenged in court, changed by future administrations, or limited by Congress.
tryItQuestion: Why might Congress let agencies make detailed rules instead of writing every detail itself?
